## [03:06 - 06:06]

your AdCopy, but the overall structure and what you measure as a conversion is rarely going to change. So we're going to focus on making regular changes to these three things. Changing your bids, excuse me, changing your bids, adding new keywords into your account and testing AdCopy. Still not working, bid it lower or still. Still not working, bid it even lower or maybe even pause that particular keyword. If it's working well, bid it up. It's pretty working. Yep, bid it up. Adding new keywords, we talked about this a little bit in the keyword module about the difference between keywords and search copies. So I'm sorry, search queries. Remember the magnet, if the search query is a close enough match to your keyword, then there's a chance that that person is going to see your Ad. And by reviewing your search query report, then you might find new keywords that you want to add into your account to be more sure that you're going to show ads to those people in the future. We also talked about negative keywords and how you might want to be on the lookout or thinking about words that you can add as a negative keyword to tell Google to not show your ad for a particular thing. We talked about the beautiful, beautiful thing that you're able to do inside of AdWords, which is to A, B test or split test. And always, always, always have at least two ads in every single ad group so that you can always be testing, which is also the name of a very, very good book, Always Be Testing. Go find that on Amazon. This is really the main benefit of Google AdWords and why it is, in my opinion, the greatest direct marketing machine ever built because we have this instant feedback loop of, I write a new ad, I come up with a new idea, a new benefit, a new headline, and I get instant feedback from the market, not from standing on the street corner with a clipboard and saying to people, oh, have a look at these two ads, please, and tell me which one you think you would click on if you saw these ads. That's opinion research. I don't like opinion research. People will tell you what they think you want to hear. That's why I'm not a massive believer in focus groups for the most part. What I like is the Court of Last Resort is showing ads to people. They don't know they're being tested, remember. People out there do not realize they're being tested. They're only seeing one ad at a time.

## [06:06 - 09:09]

And once you've got enough data, you've got something called the law of large numbers on your side. And you can see, generally speaking, when I show ad A, every 100 times I show it, 10 people click. When I show ad B, every 10 times I show it, 20 people click. All right, let's put more money behind ad B, please. Let's get rid of ad A, and let's come up with a new idea, and let's test again and test again and test again. So these are the main things that you'll be doing inside your AdWords account. Now, if you go into a search for something like Google AdWords management schedule or Google AdWords calendar, then you'll find a few blog posts that give you things like this. And this really is a fantastic thing to do. Unless you're only spending a few hundred bucks a month on Google AdWords and you're just doing it for your own business, then this is probably overkill. But if you're planning on being an agency or if you're consulting and you've got multiple clients, then having a system for your management of the AdWords accounts and knowing, generally speaking, obviously there's a bit of wiggle room with this, but knowing what you're going to be doing each day, what you need to check on, and plotting it out on something like this. And again, there's a bunch of blog posts that will give you some ideas around this. This particular one, I think I grabbed from the Lunar Metrics blog post. And this one, you can actually add to your Google Calendar if you so choose. But use it more as an idea. You don't have to do exactly this. And, oh, it says two o'clock, I must review budgets. So I must do that at two o'clock, obviously, do whatever works for you. But it is a good idea to have a system around your management. Now, we're getting towards the end of the course. So I thought of chucking in a couple more sort of advanced concepts. And one of those is the idea of quality score. So every keyword in your account is given this score between one and 10. And it is an automated opinion of how Google thinks you're going to perform that particular keyword is going to perform. Sometimes it gets it wrong. And it adjusts over time. And that score moves. You're aiming for 10 out of 10, you're probably not going to get 10 out of 10 straight out of the gate. When you're very first AdWords account, anything really over a six, you're going to be fairly happy about the game is or part of the game is to improve that quality score. Now, don't obsess over it. Quality score does not show up on your P&L. Profit is the most important measure of success. Profit ultimately is how you decide should I be doing more of this or less of this. But quality score is a really good guide. And it's pointing you in the right direction. And if you've got a bunch of keywords in your account that are ones and twos and threes, then Google is telling you, I don't think this is going to work very well. I'll keep taking your money. Thank you very much.

## [09:10 - 12:16]

I'm still going to show your ads occasionally. And I will take your money if someone clicks on your ad. But I'm going to charge you a little bit more because I don't think you're very good for my users, the people that use Google. Now, the flip side of that coin is if you've got quality scores of eight, nine or 10 out of 10, Google is essentially saying to you, thank you very much. You have come up with a keyword and an ad here that lots of people seem to like. Lots of people are clicking on your ad. They seem to be finding what they're looking for. And they're clicking on your ad all day long. Now, partly this is Google saying, thank you very much, I make lots of money from you. But really, it's about the user experience. So Google wants to reward you for being a good advertiser, because when people come to Google and find what they were looking for, and all these people are clicking on your ads, so Google is saying, well, they must be finding what they're looking for. It's going to reward you and give you what we call an Australia mates rates. So you will start to pay less and less for your clicks over time compared to other advertisers in your market. Now, if you've got quality scores of ones, twos and threes, you are likely to be paying significantly more than most advertisers in that marketplace. And so it's this sort of self correcting the genius of the system really that Google came up with 10 years ago, to reward smart advertisers and to penalize bad advertisers. So it's worth knowing how you find it inside your account. Find the keywords tab over there on the left hand side. And you'll need to click on the little columns icon. Remember, we went through that in the second module when we had a tour of the interface, I showed you the little three bars. That's where you click on to choose the columns. And you find the quality score attribute in there. Now, it may be slightly different. When you see this, you may see a whole section here where you got performance conversion attributes, competitive metrics, you may find a whole section for quality score, because they have recently changed some columns around quality score. There's actually a few more available now, since I took this screenshot. But Google haven't made those changes in this new interface just yet. Hopefully by the time you go through this course, they have so you may have to poke around a little bit there. You don't need to worry about all of those other columns. The main one that you want to turn on is just the main column for quality score. And once you do that, then you'll be able to see inside your account next to each keyword. Don't worry if you're not seeing 10s, you can be anything that's really a seven or eight or better, you'd be reasonably happy with it. Again, it's not worth obsessing about.
